However, after becoming a teacher, the resentment gradually waned. How could my teachers have focused on me when they had 50 other students in the class? How far could they have gone to be that positive influence in my life? Perhaps they were willing to help but simply lacked the energy, resources, or support to do so. Maybe that one red mark didn’t matter when they had so many other students to tend to. Now, as a teacher myself, I don’t harbour any hatred towards them. Instead, I empathize with the challenges they faced.

As I move forward in my journey to becoming an influencer, I’ve come to understand that it’s perfectly okay to be like the math tutor who was willing to reset and restart when needed. However, it’s also important to recognize that perfection lies in knowing what’s within our control and what isn’t. Our desire to influence and guide our children should never come at the cost of our mental peace, family commitments, or personal time.

One of the school heads I worked with once said, “We are not gods. We can only do our best and leave the rest to him (god).” Perhaps, in the end, my school teachers left me to him as well.

Khrawkupar Kharshiing is a passionate learner who constantly strives to learn and apply learning in the classroom. Being in the classroom makes him happy. He finds joy in reading and expressing himself through poems when he is not teaching. His poem “Caged” was awarded the tenth rank in the National Poetry competition organized by S7. He can be reached at kharshingkhraw@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Khrawkupar Kharshiing
A former “weak” student-turned-teacher, Khrawkupar Kharshiing reflects on his own struggles in the classroom and writes about the power of influence in education.

item.title: The Constitution and General Will of people
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: After our last conversation, I have been thinking about quite a few things regarding the Constitution and how we relate to it. I am still not able to wrap my head around one thing. As many as 300 people from different parts of the country came together and drafted a constitution that would constitute India as a democratic republic. How was it decided that we would be a democracy and not something else? How did these 300 people conclude that the rest of India wanted to be a democracy, when most of the population did not even know what it meant? Most importantly, they also decided on universal adult franchise!

Anubhuti: The process of drafting the Constitution itself was democratic and it was based on how the Indian society is and what it ought to be. During the debate within the Constituent Assembly, most of the members who held positions in the government were aware of the happenings in the nation. We also need to remember that the Constitution was being drafted in the midst of post-partition violence and displacement, and the concomitant effect on the way people think. They were receiving numerous letters and communication from people about the happenings in society and what ought to be done. The Constituent Assembly considered all these letters as the Will of the people. People wanted a society where such things would not happen. This required the Constituent Assembly to hypothesize the kind of nation required and have strong justifications for the same.

Ira: This almost sounds like a referendum, when the entire country’s opinion is taken into account. But I am sure many people thought otherwise. You said even within the Constituent Assembly there were disagreements, and those disagreements were voted out.

Anubhuti: Yes, Ira. Some opinions were voted out. I am sure many individual assembly members were not very happy. For instance, Maharani Amrit Kaur was not pleased with the way the right to freedom of religion was treated[i]. Some people even thought that the definition of untouchability should be limited to a religion, rather than across religions.

My point is there were disagreements on both relevant and not-so-good issues. The Assembly still had to ensure that at least a minimum number of rights and duties were enforced and that the Constitution was coherent. You could not have conflicts within the Constitution.

This reminds me of the concept of the General Will proposed by Rousseau in his book The Social Contract.

Ira: General Will? How is that different from the personal wills of individuals?

Anubhuti: Rousseau said humans were basically free and had their own wills, wants, and desires. Everyone would want to live their life on their own terms. But humans also live together in large groups, and their lives do depend on others in the society. Therefore, they could not claim to live a life that would be detrimental to the larger society or do something that others in the society would not care for or benefit from. Rousseau said that individuals would see the benefits they were getting from living in a society, and so they would cherish others in the society and would not want to go against the common ways of living. He called the will of the society the General Will, which, in a way, is the product of numerous particular wills of individuals. While they are both different, he said that they cannot be in contradiction to each other.

When the Constituent Assembly was hypothesizing what individuals in our society would want, they were actually pondering over the General Will. So, any particular suggestion made either by members of the Assembly or by citizens through their letters, would be judged against the General Will.

When a person complains about something to the government (or Constituent Assembly, which we are discussing) about something, they are saying this is how our society is organized, and we want the society to be organized differently. They are not saying we do not want these other people who we are in conflict with to live here. Rather, they are saying, find some way of organizing the society, so that problems do not crop up.

Ira: You mean to say that when people were posing societal problems in their letters to the government, they were actually pointing out how law and society should be? And this was considered as additional inputs from the society?

Anubhuti: Yes. They may not have used the terms ‘General Will’ or ‘Social Contract’, but the Constituent Assembly identified these general issues with the rights that need to be defined, the duties of citizens, and the way the government was structured and what in the structure needed to be changed. But the change should be such that it did not value the interests of some people over those of others, and yet did not allow injustice to happen. So, for instance, even if some people said there was nothing wrong with caste discrimination, the Constituent Assembly disagreed. They valued the General Will and imagined the way India ought to be more than what some particular people thought.

It was real hard work and application of logic.

Ira: But I am still confused. How did so many people at that time know that the Constitution was democratic, and it is a better way of organizing society than any other? I mean, finally a small percentage of people made these decisions.

Anubhuti: Yes, of course, every citizen did not know democracy or could not even read the Constitution. Like Madhav Khosla famously said in his book[ii], the Constitution was not only a means to dictate or prohibit actions but was also conceptualized as a pedagogical tool in order to build a new civic culture.

Over time, reconstituting India in this manner educated people in the democratic processes and the idea of how democratic societies functioned; in short, the Constitution educated us people into being democratic.

Ira: The first step would have been to educate people to vote for the right representatives and ensure they are doing the right things.

Anubhuti: Not exactly. Voting, of course, was and continues to be the most significant aspect of democracy. That is primarily because we chose the model of representative democracy. But, it is interesting that Rousseau disagreed with the idea of representation.

“One cannot transfer one’s will to another, to do with as he or she sees fit, as one does in representative democracies. Rather, the General Will depends on the coming together periodically of the entire democratic body, each and every citizen, to decide collectively, and with at least near unanimity, how to live together, i.e., what laws to enact.”[iii]

He advocated direct democracy or republicanism as it is called. Every decision is made collectively by all people, like in a referendum. Whenever a decision has to be taken, each person would have to vote and proffer their opinion on why they voted for one decision over another.

Ira: How come we call India a republic when most citizens are active only in terms of voting?

Anubhuti: That is not true. Active citizenship is demonstrated by us in various ways. Every time a significant policy is made, public acceptance is asked for. Remember a draft of the new education policy was up for review for over a month, during which an online form collected responses from all of us and changes were made to the policy before it was finalized? Similarly, for other decisions, public consultations are organized. I attended one such consultation when a decision had to be taken on whether genetically modified Brinjal seeds should be bought by the nation or not. Farmers, activists, companies, and scientists participated in this debate. And the government decided not to introduce BT-Brinjal, as it was called. Another form of active citizenship is protests. Protests are a common form of expressing our dissent against some policy or law, and the government has to reconsider policies or laws, taking into account the general will of the nation. For instance, the farm laws meant to liberalize the agricultural sector were repealed after large scale protests from farmers. All protests by groups of people may not result in policy changes, but the point of protests is active citizenship and to give voice to the General Will of the people.

In all these situations, the government has to consider the General Will of the people, and not the opinions of particular groups.

Ira: You are saying India is constituted as a democratic republic, and as of today most people have been educated into democracy by the Constitution?

Anubhuti: Of course, we all are. I would add that this knowledge and understanding is not limited to educated and literate people like us, who can read the Constitution. Most of the citizenry are aware of their rights and duties and actively demonstrate that.

I am reminded of the last civics class when a student asked me what being constituted as a democracy meant. It was tough answering that question. I found the meaning of the word in a dictionary, which says, “The structure, composition, physical makeup, or nature of something” very useful.

When we use this definition in relation to the body and mind, we are referring to our overall health. When we use this for a society, we mean the nature of the society. India was a British colony until 1947 and we transformed into a democratic republic in 1950.

You are right in that it seems like a gamble to form a democratic state that demands so much from citizens most of who were not literate or educated in 1950. But the Constituent Assembly was right in accepting that the Constitution would serve as a pedagogical tool to educate us into a democratic culture. Our parents and grandparents seem to have done a good job, don’t you think?

[i]Episode 5 of Samvidhan TV series. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0U9KDQnIsNk&t=33s

[ii]Introduction of “India’s Founding Moment: The Constitution of a Most Surprising Democracy” by Madhav Khosla

[iii]Entry on “Social Contract Theory” in the Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/soc-cont/

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
How did 300 people decide India's democratic fate? As the conversation between Ira and Anubhuti continues, Prakash Iyer shares how the Constitution shaped our society.

item.title: Stopping the [doom]scroll
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

In the ever-evolving world of technology, one of marketing's latest mantras is “stop the scroll.” This credo highlights the reality of how deeply scrolling is ingrained in our daily lives. Whether it’s social media, short-form videos, or news feeds, the act of scrolling has become a reflex, a habit tied to our thumbs, and increasingly, to our minds. While advertisers see this as an opportunity to grab attention, educators must grapple with the phenomenon of “doomscrolling” – a behaviour that has individuals compulsively scrolling through negative or distressing content online. This issue is especially concerning for schoolchildren, who, instead of engaging in healthy learning or social activities, are consumed by endless scrolling. The implications for mental health, focus, and overall wellbeing are profound.

To understand the impact of scrolling on children, it’s essential to unpack the changes it brings to the human mind. Research on attention spans, although contested, consistently suggests a decline. Some studies claim the average attention span has reduced from 12 seconds to as low as eight seconds in the social media age. Regardless of the exact number, the conclusion is clear: the sheer volume of content and its algorithmic delivery have reshaped how young minds process information. Social media platforms are designed to provide endless personalized feeds, which while engaging, can also be overwhelming. For children, this poses unique challenges. Their brains are still developing, making them particularly susceptible to the dopamine hits that come with every scroll, like, or share. This constant stimulation can make it difficult for them to focus on tasks requiring sustained attention, such as reading, problem-solving, or participating in class discussions. Moreover, the personalized nature of feeds means they’re often exposed to echo chambers or distressing content tailored to their fears or insecurities, feeding into the doomscrolling cycle.

Doomscrolling refers to the compulsive consumption of negative news or content, often leaving individuals feeling anxious or helpless. For children, this behaviour is particularly concerning. A newsfeed filled with climate change disasters, political unrest, or personal insecurities amplified by social media comparisons can contribute to feelings of anxiety, depression, and eco-anxiety. These emotions, when unchecked, seep into classrooms, affecting academic performance and peer relationships. There is a growing trend of students discussing catastrophic events they read about online, from wildfires to global pandemics. These conversations may not be rooted in classroom lessons but may stem from the headlines they encountered during late-night scrolling. There has been an uptick in students expressing fears about the future after spending hours consuming content about economic instability and climate crises.

Scrolling is not inherently bad. It offers access to a wealth of information, sparks creativity, and provides new ways to connect. However, the medium often dictates the message. Short-form content, designed for quick consumption, prioritizes entertainment over depth. For students, this can lead to surface-level engagement rather than deep learning. Moreover, the fragmented nature of scrolling affects how children process information. They’re trained to skim rather than analyze, making it harder to develop critical thinking skills. The constant influx of new content leaves little room for reflection or synthesis, key components of meaningful education. Teachers often find themselves competing for attention in a world where every piece of information is designed to “stop the scroll.” There is a growing struggle to focus on long-form reading assignments in class. Many admit they are accustomed to skimming social media posts and find it difficult to engage with dense texts.

While schools cannot shield students entirely from the digital world, they can equip them with tools to navigate it in a healthy way. Teaching students to critically evaluate the content they encounter online is a vital step. Discussing how algorithms work and why certain content appears in their feeds can help them distinguish between credible sources and sensationalized material. By understanding the mechanics behind social media, students can become more discerning consumers. Introducing a digital literacy module in school where students analyze their social media feeds, identifying clickbait headlines, and exploring how algorithms tailor content to individual users, would be a first step. This exercise would not only educate them but also spark conversations about responsible consumption.

Introducing students to the concept of mindfulness in their digital habits is another crucial strategy. Encouraging them to set boundaries, such as designated screen-free times or breaks from social media, can make a significant difference. Tools like screen time trackers or apps that limit usage can be helpful. Mindfulness practices like meditation or journalling can also counterbalance the constant stimulation of digital devices. Keeping a “scrolling diary” for a week, documenting how much time they spent online and how it made them feel would likely be an eye-opening exercise for both students and teachers.

Providing a platform for students to share their thoughts and feelings about the content they encounter online can also help. Classroom discussions or counselling sessions allow them to process their emotions and understand that they’re not alone in feeling overwhelmed. Weekly “Media Monday” sessions, where students could discuss news stories or trends they had seen online would not only build critical thinking skills but also help students articulate their concerns and support one another.

Counteracting the effects of short-form content by incorporating activities that require sustained focus is essential. Encouraging students to read books, engage in debates, or work on projects that demand critical thinking and creativity highlights the value of depth over breadth in learning. Replacing traditional tests with project-based assignments, asking students to research historical events and present their findings is sure to lead them to deeper engagement and collaborative learning. Teachers can also lead by example, demonstrating balanced digital habits and sharing their own strategies for managing screen time or finding uplifting content. When students see adults navigating the digital world responsibly, they’re more likely to follow suit.

Rather than banning devices, integrating them into the learning process in constructive ways can show students that technology can be a force for good. Educational apps, collaborative platforms, or digital storytelling tools can highlight stories of innovation and resilience to counter the negativity in their feeds.

Teachers cannot address doomscrolling by themselves. Collaboration with parents and the broader community is essential. Schools can organize workshops for parents to recognize and address doomscrolling behaviours. Community programs can provide resources and support for children struggling with digital addiction or anxiety. Parents, in particular, play a crucial role. They can monitor their children’s online activity, set household rules for screen time, and engage in conversations about their digital experiences. Encouraging offline activities, such as sports, arts, or volunteering, can provide children with alternative sources of fulfilment and joy.

In a world designed to keep us scrolling, teaching children to “stop the scroll” is an act of empowerment. By addressing the phenomenon of doomscrolling and promoting healthier digital habits, teachers can help students reclaim their focus, foster critical thinking, and build emotional resilience. While the digital age brings challenges, it also offers opportunities to rethink how we engage with information and each other. As educators, the goal is not to resist technology but to guide students in using it wisely, ensuring that scrolling becomes a tool for growth rather than a trap of negativity.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a consultant on working with GenZ; Leading with Social Impact, and Suicide Prevention and Destigmatisation. A millennial in spirit, with the benefit of hindsight, she uses her 37 years in media and education to help leverage generational diversity at work and at home. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
How does endless scrolling affect children’s focus, learning, and emotions? Neerja Singh shares some digital habits that can encourage deep learning in the age of short-form content.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Thank you for such an enlightening article. I really appreciate the new terms used to describe and analyse the new digital practices and obsessions. Awareness is the key.

item.title: Dear teacher, I have so much to say
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Bhuvaneswari B and Sapna Bhat

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) are neurodevelopmental conditions accompanied by differences in brain development. ADHD is characterized by difficulties with attention and/or hyperactivity and impulsivity. ASD as defined by Diagnostic and Statistical Manual 5* is characterized by enduring and impairing difficulties in social communication/interaction and restrictive and repetitive behaviours, interests, and activities, or sensory abnormalities. These two conditions usually co-occur. Mainstream schools can be difficult for such children for a variety of reasons. The following is a fictional description of a child who has been diagnosed with these disorders and what he might tell his teachers to help him succeed in school.

D is a curious 11 year-old boy with autism and ADHD in a general education classroom. He is the single child of very accomplished parents who care deeply about his wellbeing. D loves to read anything he gets his hands on, however he particularly enjoys reading about science, history, and computers. He loves coding, board games, and art. He finds physical activities difficult and is very aware of the differences in his abilities in comparison to those of his peers. He is above grade level on reading, maths, and writing. However, it is sometimes difficult for him to work on these subjects in the classroom as he finds it distracting and occasionally overwhelming. D thinks social situations and friendships are puzzling and in his own words he thinks he has “dyslexia for social situations”. He is aware of his ADHD diagnosis but doesn’t know that he has autism. D has a hard time with self regulation and being able to use learnt strategies when his emotions get out of control, such as raising his hand, and being able to keep his thoughts in his head. He also has intrusive thoughts about peers, which he finds difficult to control including thoughts like “friends ganging up”, “peers spying”, etc., and this interferes with his functioning in the classroom and on the playground. D’s brilliant mind and creativity remain hidden under the label of bad behaviour and perceived rudeness and disrespect. His peers avoid being paired with him in group activities and his teachers find it difficult to deal with his dysregulation when teaching a big class.

D loves school and his teachers, and wants to make friends. With the new school year starting, he wanted a change in his situation at school. At the behest of his parents, special education teacher, and speech language therapist, he set out to write this letter to help his teachers understand him.

DEAR TEACHER, I HAVE SO MUCH TO SAY...

I MAY NOT LOOK AT YOU WHILE YOU SPEAK. BUT I AM LISTENING.

Eye contact can be overwhelming for me, and it takes a lot of energy to process everything happening around me. Even when I'm not looking at you, I am paying attention to your words.

I MAY NOT ALWAYS USE SPEECH TO TALK TO YOU… BUT I HAVE SO MUCH TO SAY.

My thoughts and feelings are just as rich and complex as anyone else's. I use a device (computer or tablet) sometimes to share my ideas and needs. Your patience and encouragement mean a lot to me.

I SOMETIMES TAKE A LONG TIME TO ANSWER… BUT I AM TRYING.

Formulating responses and using my communication devices can take extra time. Please wait for me and give me the space I need to express myself. You can say something like, “D, it will be your turn after B’s and C’s.”

I FEEL VERY ANXIOUS WHEN THE ROUTINE IS UNPREDICTABLE. BUT I WANT TO PARTICIPATE.

Changes in routine can make me feel lost and anxious. A consistent schedule helps me feel safe and ready to engage in activities. When things change, it helps if you can prepare me in advance.

I LIKE TO USE PICTURES TO UNDERSTAND AND COMMUNICATE.

Visual supports like pictures and written words help me make sense of instructions and concepts. They also help me express my thoughts and feelings more clearly.

I DON’T LIKE MELTDOWNS EITHER, BUT MY BODY AND MIND DON’T ALWAYS WORK TOGETHER TO LET ME EXPRESS WHAT I MEAN.

When I have a meltdown, it’s often because I’m overwhelmed or can’t communicate my needs effectively. Your understanding and calm during these times help me feel better. Please give me a safe space to calm down, don’t ask me too many questions or ask me to problem solve at this point, it can make me feel more overwhelmed.

I WANT TO LEARN, I WANT TO LISTEN.

I am excited to be in your class and to learn new things. Your support and understanding help me participate and succeed.

I LIKE WORKING WITH OTHERS, SO HELP ME BY SETTING THE GROUP RULES.

Groups get disorganized for me sometimes, please choose my group-mates carefully. Setting some ground rules for the group will help us function well together.

SENSORY STIMULUS LIKE NOISE AND BRIGHT LIGHTS CAN OVERWHELM ME.

Sometimes I might need noise blockers, chewy toys, or a quiet space to function well. Initially, I might need you to offer me these choices and later on I might gradually begin advocating for my needs.

MY BRAIN GETS TIRED QUICKLY AND I CAN GET DISTRACTED WHEN MY BRAIN IS TIRED. Encourage me to take brain breaks and help me set timers for these breaks. Brain breaks help me stay focused.

I AM VERY PASSIONATE ABOUT CERTAIN TOPICS AND ENJOY TALKING ABOUT THEM AT LENGTH.

You can help me by listening when you can, and let me know when you don’t have the time to listen. You can say, “I am really interested in what you have to say, but I don’t have the time now. Let’s find a time when I am free and can listen to you.” This validates my feelings and helps me understand that my thoughts are important. This will help reduce my anxiety and calm me down.

IF YOU SEE ME MAKING REPETITIVE MOVEMENTS WITH MY BODY, PLEASE DON’T GET SCARED.

I use repeated body movements like rocking, flapping, clapping, etc., to express my emotions, especially to indicate that I am excited.

I HAVE MY STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES, PLEASE APPRECIATE AND ENGAGE MY STRENGTHS TOO.

Even though my weaknesses might catch your attention, please try to identify my strengths as well. I engage well when you identify and utilize my strengths like coding, music, math, etc., to teach me.

THANK YOU FOR READING,

YOURS SINCERELY,

D.

ASD and ADHD can present unique challenges in a general education classroom. Traditional methods of teaching and classroom management may not always meet the needs of such children. D’s letter to his teacher helps us understand the way children with ASD perceive and interact with the world around them. D’s letter reminds us that, with appropriate support, children like D can thrive socially and academically in general education classrooms. Teachers need to ensure that such children’s strengths are not overshadowed by their challenges. Teachers play an important role, not just in the educational journey of children on the spectrum, but also in making them feel heard, valued, and understood.

* The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) is used by clinicians and researchers to diagnose and classify mental disorders.

Bhuvaneswari is a faculty member at the School of Education, Azim Premji University. A Speech Language Therapist by training, she has an MPhil and PhD from the Department of Studies in Psychology, University of Mysore. Her clinical work and teaching covers various domains such as early intervention, language acquisition and disorders, clinical linguistics, learning disabilities, autism spectrum disorders, and other neurodiversities. She teaches in the Early Childhood Education programme and the programme on Teaching Children with Learning Disabilities at Azim Premji University, apart from anchoring the disability inclusion activities of the University.

Sapna Bhat is a speech language therapist who consults with different international schools in Greater Copenhagen in Denmark. She has a PhD from the All India institute of speech and hearing, Mysore and has 24 years of experience working as an educator, researcher, and a clinician in the field of speech and language therapy. She is passionate about neuro-inclusion and works closely with special educators and families to facilitate inclusive education and scaffold the social world for neurodivergent children.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Bhuvaneswari B and Sapna Bhat
What if a struggling student could tell you exactly what they need? Bhuvaneswari B and Sapna Bhat pen a letter addressed to teachers from a student with ASD and ADHD, sharing his hopes, challenges, and dreams.

item.title: Living with microbes
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

After the excitement of seeing fungi under the microscope, all of us were waiting to see bacteria. Jayanthi called us to a desk. “Come here and observe these slides,” she said.

As we walked over to Jayanthi’s desk, Sujatha noticed something and asked, “Jayanthi, why are you keeping this spoiled carrot here?”

“We use the spoiled carrot to prepare bacterial culture. Look through the microscope. Can you see the orange spots? These bacteria are long, rod-shaped and are called ‘Erwinia’. They also attack other vegetables like potato, tomato, and cucumber,” Jayanthi said.

“It is plant bacteria. What are the other kinds of bacteria we commonly find?” Rini asked.

“Lactobacillus is from milk. I diluted and strained some buttermilk to show it to you. Lactobacilli are among the most common probiotics found in foods such as curd. They help us maintain our health, and can treat diarrhea and skin disorders such as eczema.”

“Oh! I bought probiotic bottles from the market. Now I know why our elders ask us to consume buttermilk or curd regularly,” Madhavi said.

“Have you heard of ‘Escherichia coli’or ‘E. coli’? It is part of the microbiota of our gut. It is normally found in the lower intestine of animals, including humans. Some strains of E. coli benefit their hosts by producing vitamin K. They also prevent colonization of harmful bacteria in the intestine. This is representative of a mutual relationship between bacteria and humans,” Jayanthi said.

Madhavi said, “But E. coli contaminated water causes stomach ache and nausea. I read it somewhere.”

“Yes, some strains of E. coli are pathogenic. Open defecation is the main reason for ground and surface water pollution, including streams, rivers, lakes, and water used to irrigate crops. So, we must wash our hands with soap and water every time we use the toilet,” I replied.

“Before eating food, we should wash our hands to avoid infections,” Vivan added.

“Bacteria also enter through open wounds and cause diseases. An infected wound can swell, become red, feel warm, and ooze pus. Pneumonia is caused by Streptococcus pneumonia, which colonizes the nasal cavity and respiratory tract,” Raja said.

“A few days back, while cycling I hurt my leg. My father washed the wound with running water. He used salt water to clean around the wound, then covered the wound with a bandage. I got cured easily,” Vivan said.

“Indeed, that is the right way to treat wounds. This avoids bacterial and fungal infections,” Raja said.

“How do bacteria reproduce?” Rini asked.

“Look at these pictures. Bacteria reproduce in different ways. When the cell grows to a maximum size, it divides into two identical cells. This is called binary fission. When a cell grows and divides into many cells, it is called fragmentation. When one side of the cell grows and separates, it is called budding.”

“There is another method too. Two cells come together and form a cytoplasmic bridge. The cell content flows between the two and then the cell separates. This is called conjugation,” Jayanthi explained.

“I have a question. Though the place contaminated by microbes is completely cleaned, again they reappear in the next season. I noticed it on walls. How does that happen?” Anand asked.

“During unfavourable conditions, like a dry season, or when there is scarcity of food, the cells dry up and form a thick wall to protect the microbes from harsh environmental conditions. When the conditions are favourable again, these spores begin to germinate and start their normal activities,” Raja replied.

“We observed only rod-shaped bacteria. Do they exist in other shapes also?” Anand asked.

“Bacteria occur in different types and shapes in Nature. See this poster. Cocci are spherical in shape. Others may be spiral, club, or comma-shaped. In addition to this, some may have single or many flagella,” Raja pointed to the poster hanging on the wall.

“Yes, we observed rod-shaped bacteria in chains,” Vivan said.

Prof. Reshma entered the lab and asked us how things were going on.

Anand replied, “From the pond water sample, we observed organisms under the microscope. Paramecium was moving; we also saw some round green algae.”

“Ma’am, we observed penicillium growing on lemon and hairs on fern leaves,” Sujatha replied.

“Different types of bacteria also,” Rini added.

“Very good. You have seen Cycas trees in the garden. Here, in the lab, you will see the cross-section of a Cycas root. The central portion of the root is called the ‘pith.’ Surrounding the pith is the cortex region. You can notice green algal colonies in the cortex,” Reshma said.

“These are coralloid roots. I remember Nandini aunty once informed us about this,” Shilpa said.

“Yes, algae help Cycas to fix atmospheric nitrogen in root cells,” Reshma added.

Jayanthi called everyone to observe a cross-section of lichen and coralloid roots.

“This is great! Algae and fungi are so fragile and they made a new life together called Lichen. Is it not?” Sujatha asked.

“Yes, it's amazing. If we look at the evolution of life, algae originated earlier and Cycas later. How does algae enter the roots of Cycas and start living there? Similarly, how did the E. coli and human relationship start?” Anand asked.

“It's interesting. If you study evolutionary biology, you will learn more about the different types of relationships among various creatures in this world,” I replied.

“It's a lot of information to process and we will need time,” Madhavi said.

Sujatha folded her hands and said, “Reshma ma’am, thanks a lot for letting us into your department and showing the botanical garden and lab to all of us,”

“Oh! It is my pleasure. I hope the children will remember what they saw here today when they go to higher classes,” Reshma replied.

“Thank you Jayanthi and Raja. I appreciate your efforts to make us understand the ‘microbe’ world,” Madhavi thanked everyone.

Suggested activities

  1. Identify different types of probiotics found in the kitchen.
  2. Make a list of diseases caused by bacteria in human beings.

References

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacteria
  • https://isappscience.org/for-scientists/resources/probiotics/
  • https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/e-coli/symptoms-causes/syc-20372058

Nandini Dholepat is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
The Backyard Discovery group learns about the many roles of bacteria and how they shape our world: there are some that heal, others that harm, and quite a few that even help plants grow.

item.title: Mountains of lessons
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mala Kumar

One of the most well-known government buildings in India, the Rashtrapati Bhavan, sits atop a hill. The grand home of the country’s president is built on the part of the Aravalli Range called the Delhi Ridge. And what is special about the Aravalli Range? With some rocks in it as old as 3,200 million years, the formation of the range started some 1800 million years ago. It is the oldest mountain range in India, and sadly, the most eroded too. Apart from the natural erosion process, humans too have continuously mined these gentle mountains, built on them, and hunted through their forests.

For most of us who do not live on the mountains or anywhere close to a hill, it is difficult to see them as living ecosystems that are integral to our lives. Behind every glass of water that we drink is the fact that it probably reached us after emerging from and flowing down a mountain. Our wellbeing depends, to a large extent, on the produce we get from the mountains, directly or indirectly. How can we as adults help our children understand the importance of mountains? An immersive excursion to a mountainous area would be the best way, but a close second would be to dedicate at least a week to learning from the mountains. Full disclosure: I am a writer, not a full-time teacher, although I have been in many classrooms as a facilitator engaging in nonformal teaching. After my book Up the Mountains of India was published, I’ve also had occasion to conduct many sessions based on the book with students in different schools, giving me some confidence in suggesting the following activities. If the school timetable allows some flexibility, maybe teachers could swap classes so that the activities can be held for two continuous periods each day, for six-to-seven days. If not, one period a day would be a good starting point too.

(This article includes a suggested list of reading via the ‘Nature Writing for Children Newsletter: https://azimpremjiuniversity.edu.in/articles-nwfc/mountain-magic).

Before the week begins, students have to be assigned the task of reading as much about mountains as possible. Subject teachers need to use one period during that week for the mountain masterclass. Let’s begin.

Day 1: Language class, let’s say Kannada

Students carry chits of paper and pencils. Have the class sit in any place that has steps – an amphitheater outdoors or the school auditorium would be great, but a staircase would be equally effective for the experiment. The aim of the game is for students on the top step to write a line in Kannada (or any other language used in the region) about the most amazing thing they read about mountains. (Depending on the age and exposure of the children to the topic, the teacher could hint that it could be related to anything. It could be something like ‘the Himalaya is a young mountain range’, or that six of the world’s staple food crops grow best on mountains – potatoes, maize, apples, tomatoes, sorghum, and barley). As the sheet is passed down, students add more interesting bits related to mountains in the language being studied and pass it down. Students on the bottom-most step read out the lines.

Like with a game of Chinese Whispers, this game may lead to some mirth and noise, and I think it would be totally worth it. Years ago, I had spent a few minutes gazing at a group of children sitting in the Panna Meena kund (also called Panna Miyah kund) in Jaipur. Were the children counting the number of sets of steps going down to the stepwell? Were they wondering at the geometry of the place? Were they amazed with the symmetry of the square structure a short distance away from the famous Amer Fort in the Aravalli mountains? All around the stepwell were stairs that for hundreds of years had served not just as a means to reach the water, but also as a place to sit and spend time with friends and family, discussing matters of importance, or engaging in the important activity of relaxing and doing nothing.

Expected outcome: Students improve their vocabulary in a particular language. They write in the language being studied. The final result is a set of short essays on the topic of mountains. If nothing, the students have relaxed for some time, making them more open to learning new things!

Mountain words: Another activity during a language class could be about mountain words. Is a hill the same as a mountain?

Till the 1970s, a mountain was a landform that rose above 1000 feet. Anything under was called a hill. But now, the two words are used interchangeably, though a hill still means something small and a mountain, big. So, what does making a mountain out of a molehill mean? Is a peak the same as a summit? Can you recall any phrases that feature a mountain-related word? How about these – peak form, mountain of dishes, uphill task, going downhill, if the mountain won't go to Mohammed, then Mohammed must come to the Mountain?

Other activities in a language class could be reading from the works of writers in the hills (Rudyard Kipling, Ruskin Bond) or reading creative work about mountains.

Day 2: Social studies

Open the class to questions. What are tectonic plates? How are mountains formed? How do they affect the climate? What are the different kinds of mountains? Can volcanoes erupt from any mountain? What are the examples of wildlife in, say, the Eastern Ghats? Continue with this range as the chosen one for further activities.

This will be a theory class, where students just have to come up with questions. Alternatively, make it a quiz session with each group asking another a question. Questions get 2 points. Answers get 4. At the end of the class, there are likely to be several unanswered questions. Farm the questions to students, so that each student (or a pair of students) needs to get the answer to just one question. If a library period can be used, that will be useful. If not, this can be given as an after-school assignment.

Day 3: Science

With all the information collected as part of the social studies class, students will now probably be able to arrange it under different heads – history, mathematics, physics, chemistry, and so on. If some topics haven’t come out during the fact-finding exercises, say about hot geysers in cold mountains, the teacher could introduce the topic. How do some pilgrims to Yamunotri in Uttarakhand cook their rice? They dip a bundle of raw rice into a natural hot spring! Does it take longer to cook rice in the plains or in the mountains? Why? Does the time vary depending on where the mountain is? If possible, teachers could show pictures of several mountains to showcase different topics in science – the rainbow hills in Ladakh and the marble rocks of Bhedaghat in the Satpura range (minerals and the formation of colours), Spiti’s shales (fossilization of species that died maybe a 100 million years ago), the clouded leopards of Mizo Hills (long fat tails and the physics of balance), sky islands of the Western Ghats (cloud formation) and so on. A visual tour and science class rolled into one!

Day 4: Mathematics

Students of grade 6 and up probably know that Mount Everest is the highest mountain on Earth, but Mauna Kea in Hawaii is the tallest. Ask the students how they can show this in class. (Hint: Time for a student to stand up on the bench!) Talk about standard measurements and why we use them in various professions. In the age before satellites, drones, and GPS, how did a mathematician who never climbed a mountain calculate the exact height of Mt. Everest? Could the class calculate the height of a tree in the school compound without climbing it? Depending on the grade, there are many math activities that could be done using mountain data – ascending and descending order, conversion of units from one system to another, mountain economics (like, why are some vegetables costlier in the hills than in the plains?), what are the different shapes of mountains (sharp pyramids of the Karakoram, table mountains, dome-shaped ones, conical volcanic ones, and so on)?

Day 5: Society and ethics

The most important part of learning about mountains is to make children understand the interconnected world we live in. Already, within a person’s lifetime, we are seeing the effect of deforestation and increased use of fossil fuels. Deforestation in mountains, specifically, is a major cause of landslides and floods. Along with this, there is a huge loss of endemic species. To show issues like human-wildlife conflict, construction versus destruction, and other ethical issues, it would be useful to have a role play session. Consider giving the following roles to different students: student in a hill school, farmer in a remote mountain trying to sell his vegetables in a market, a citizen in a mountain town (Ketti/Darjeeling/Mount Abu/Leh), an animal rights activist in a metro, a swamp deer or barasingha, a snow leopard, and so on. Each character makes a move, and the others have to decide whether it will affect them or not. For example, the mountain farmer asks his local government to build a road from their village across the national park to the city. Who does it affect and how? Who is right? Who is wrong? It is hoped that the students may see not just how each is connected to the other, but also how communication across stakeholders is important to find solutions.

Life skills can be a part of this class too. Some years ago, I was part of a group that interacted with children in a tiny school in a Himalayan village. We asked them to list out their problems big and small. An illustrator got them to draw what they had talked about. They also picked up one problem – too many plastic water bottles thrown away by tourists – and came up with ideas to upcycle them. Within a week, the children had drawn fruit-plucking tools, water filters, and irrigators using plastic bottles. The entire exercise became the content for the picture book I wrote called How to Solve a Problem Like Himani (Pratham Books).

Day 6: Art

Continue with the chosen range, the Eastern Ghats. The aim is to use an art form like collage to show a diagrammatic representation of a mountain range. A large pinboard would be a great way to showcase this work of group art. If a wall can be used, it would be ideal. Old newspaper works well for the pinboard or wall. Any area, at least 150 cm by 300 cm, on the floor would work too, in which case, it would be a drawing on the floor with chalk instead of making a collage.

Organize the students into groups. Share with them the idea that the drawings can be raw and representative (like the rock paintings in the Bhimbetka caves in the Vindhya Range), and need not be detailed and time-consuming (like the araash fresco work in Rajasthan’s palaces in the Aravalli Range), and just a couple of each to start with. Group 1 makes the mountains (say, by tearing newspaper sheets to get six large triangles to represent mountains). Group 2 makes the flora (teak tree?) Group 3 makes the fauna (fishing cat!). Group 4 makes rivers. Group 5 sticks them to the mountains. If the students are using the floor or wall to draw, then the work could be done by one group after another. Placing the paper trees, rivers, animals on the drawing of the mountains would work as well as drawing them on the mountains.

Day 7: Music and theatre

Students can find songs about mountains and our natural world to sing in class. Many folk songs talk about mountains and rivers, like Manne nambi yelelo sung by the Irula tribe in the Western Ghats, or modern songs like In This Together by Ellie Golding. Wouldn’t it be wonderful to adapt songs about our natural world for annual day performances? The Gillo Repertory Theatre brought out a wonderful play last year based on the book The Ghost of the Mountains. Poet Nirmala Putul writes in her native Santali and in Hindi. Her poem Mountain Child can convey many aspects of these landforms – natural beauty, the curiosity of children towards what they see, and more.

Conclusion

To wrap up, there are many things that children can do to understand the importance of mountains. Hopefully, students will read the suggested list of activities compiled towards the end of Up the Mountains of India. Projects are a great way for students to learn about things. All of us, and students specifically, are surrounded by too much information. I hope a project like this imparts something that is almost selfish in nature – that we need mountains for our wellbeing. That, if each of us wants a better world for ourselves, we have to let our natural resources be. We have to sustain them. Our mountains are ours – to safeguard, and to use wisely.

Mala Kumar is an independent editor and the author of Travelling Treasures, the Rupaiya Paisa series, I want that one!, and many other books for children. A former editor with Pratham Books, she can be reached at mala.kumar@yahoo.co.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mala Kumar
What if every glass of water you drank had a story that began in the mountains? Mala Kumar’s interdisciplinary classroom project will help children understand why mountains matter.

item.title: Seeing ‘Missed Takes’ in mistakes
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

“There is no room for mistakes in my classroom!” These words from her own school teacher have remained in the mind of an experienced teacher of English (and a Head of department today) in a leading school in Hyderabad.

Accustomed to a culture that condemned mistakes and upheld the practice of never committing any error, this teacher (when she turned into one herself) made a few discoveries at an advanced stage of her teaching career: and it all happened through a course that she enrolled in.

Titled SEEING MISSED TAKES IN MISTAKES, she affirmed that this course helped her get into the minds of her students. How did this happen? She found that her students began articulating their thought processes while committing errors, as they started examining the errors that they had made. Even though her years of experience as a teacher had inevitably included correcting students’ mistakes – and even discussing them occasionally – she candidly admitted that until she took this course, she had never really regarded errors as resources: which first required setting the ground for an honest and comfortable discussion of errors.

“It is not an easy task to own up to mistakes! I still remember my English teacher’s dictum, after all!” She laughingly confessed. And then, she added profoundly: “If we condemn making mistakes, students will begin to condemn learning.”

So how did this teacher go about her revelatory process? Firstly, she viewed mistakes as a very natural part of the learning process, instead of condemning them. Beginning with an honest admission of the mistakes that she would make when she was a student herself – and how she had often frustrated her own teachers with her repetitive patterns of errors – she found that hesitant students slowly began to open up and confess their own error patterns, with less and less shame or hesitation.

Gradually, she worked with another teacher in her English department to share her own learnings from the course. Then, she even went so far as to encourage that teacher to put into practice some of the strategies that she had acquired through the course.

So did this demand too much of that teacher’s time? Surprisingly, not for too long, she confessed. Starting with crafting dummy worksheets, where she deliberately made the same types of errors that her students usually made, she then got her students to check these worksheets – which she claimed she had sourced from a similar grade of another school.

Naturally, as they were now sitting in the chair of a teacher, her fourth graders had great fun.

“It was a laughter riot,” declared the teacher, “nonetheless, with a lot of learning!”

They began exclaiming:

“Even I make these errors, ma’am!”

“These students are not even proof-reading their work before turning it in!”

All this happened within one 40-minute period. Now that they had freely examined another’s errors, they were more open to revisiting their own – with an open mind.

“Can you sit with me for five minutes, ma’am? I keep making mistakes with the use of articles…please show me how to get over this!”

This, declared the teacher, was a paradigm shift: when students began to open their minds to metacognition. “Why did I think thus? How can I revisit my own thinking?”

The focus then shifted from merely distributing remedial worksheets to asking why certain errors are made – and often repeatedly. Not surprisingly, student performance improved significantly within a short timeframe of five months: starting from noticeably better handwriting and timely submission of work, to actual drop in the number of repetitive errors.

She sums up her own shift thus: “Today, I see myself as a teacher-educator who welcomes mistakes in my class. Because, for me, I see the need to move beyond right and wrong and use mistakes as a resource to probe thought processes.”

Watch her video here: https://youtu.be/vykX9f45XpI?si=Ew4f17jX3BecABtX

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What works better: correcting mistakes or learning from them? Neeraja Raghavan writes about how a simple mindset shift turned mistakes into stepping stones for deeper understanding.

item.title: The extracurricular kid: nurturing talent beyond academics
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

The anxiety of parents and the struggle of schools

Every parent dreams of their child excelling in school – good grades, appreciation from teachers, and a promising future. But what happens when the child struggles with academics and instead shines in a sport, music, painting, dance, or some other skill? Many parents experience anxiety, fearing that their child's future might be at risk due to poor grades. Meanwhile, schools, bound by rigid curricula, often fail to accommodate and nurture these children in a way that truly recognizes their strengths. The result? A cycle of self-doubt and frustration for the child and parents alike.

The Indian education system, for the most part, still prioritizes textbook knowledge over talent-based learning. While some progressive institutions and alternative schools support artistic and sports-oriented children, mainstream education often falls short in fostering and honing special skills while ensuring that children are not made to feel like failures for struggling in academics.

So, how do parents and educators deal with this dilemma? How can a child’s natural abilities be encouraged while ensuring that essential academic knowledge isn’t completely ignored? And most importantly, when and how should a child consider making their talent a career choice?

Identifying the special skill

Recognizing a child's talent early is crucial. This doesn’t necessarily mean waiting for them to win a medal or a competition but observing where they naturally excel.

  1. Interest and enjoyment: A child who is deeply engrossed in sketching for hours, tapping rhythms on tables, or spending every free moment on a football field is displaying signs of passion.
  2. Effortless skill: Some children grasp musical notes, dance moves, or sports techniques faster than others, often without formal training.
  3. Recognition from others: Teachers, coaches, or even family members may notice and acknowledge the child’s unique abilities.

Parents should engage their child in various activities early on and observe what excites them most. Allowing the child to explore activities that interest them, without forcing them into pre-determined career paths can lead to genuine talent discovery.

Methods to hone the skill

Once a child’s special skill is identified, nurturing it is the next step. This requires structured efforts from parents, educators, and mentors.

  1. Formal training: Enrol the child in professional coaching, workshops, or specialized classes. A good mentor can guide skill development in a structured manner.
  2. Practice and exposure: The 10,000-hour rule suggests that mastery comes with consistent practice. Encourage daily efforts and provide exposure through competitions, exhibitions, or performances.
  3. Financial and emotional support: Specialized training, equipment, and travel for competitions can be expensive. More than financial investment, emotional support and encouragement from parents are vital for confidence-building.
  4. Balancing pressure: While discipline is important, excessive pressure can backfire. Encourage progress, but don’t turn passion into an obligation.

Marrying basic academics with skill development

While academic excellence may not be the strong suit of an extracurricular-driven child, basic literacy and numeracy are essential in today’s world. The challenge is to create a balance where academics do not overshadow talent but still remain a part of learning.

  1. Customized learning plans: Schools can offer flexible academic programs where core subjects (math, language, and science) are taught in ways suited to different learning styles.
  2. Skill-based curriculum: Alternative schools like Rishi Valley and The Valley School blend skill development with basic education, allowing children to thrive in both areas.
  3. Technology as a tool: Online learning platforms allow children to study at their own pace, reducing the pressure of traditional schooling while ensuring academic progress.
  4. Open communication: Parents and schools must collaborate to create a supportive ecosystem, where the child doesn’t feel isolated for performing poorly in traditional academics.

When to make the special skill a career choice

This is one of the biggest dilemmas for both children and parents. Making an unconventional skill a full-time career is daunting, especially given societal norms that prioritize traditional professions like engineering, medicine, or law. Here’s when a talent can be considered a viable career:

  1. Consistent excellence: If a child consistently outperforms others in their skill area and shows a rapid learning curve, it indicates career potential.
  2. Recognition and opportunity: Winning state or national-level competitions, being scouted for professional training, or receiving opportunities in their field are strong signs.
  3. Sustainable earning possibility: Researching income opportunities in the field and understanding financial sustainability is crucial. With platforms like YouTube, online teaching, freelancing, and sports scholarships, monetization of talent is more accessible than before.
  4. Passion over pressure: If the child sees their talent not just as a hobby but as something they genuinely want to dedicate their life to, it’s worth pursuing.

Indian examples of special skill careers

  1. P.T. Usha (Athletics): Born in a modest family, she pursued running despite the lack of sports infrastructure in her village. Today, she is one of India’s most celebrated athletes.
  2. Lydian Nadhaswaram (Music): A young piano prodigy from Chennai, he won the international reality show "The World's Best" at the age of 13, bringing global recognition to Indian musical talent.
  3. Biswa Kalyan Rath (Stand-up comedy): An IIT graduate, he pursued comedy despite societal expectations of a conventional job, proving that creativity can be a career.
  4. Rameshbabu Praggnanandhaa (Chess): At just 16, he has defeated world champions and shown that chess, often seen as an extra activity, can be a career path.
  5. Sudarsan Pattnaik (Sand art): Despite being from a poor background, his perseverance turned sand art into a globally recognized skill, earning him multiple awards.

Conclusion

Not every child is meant to be a doctor or an engineer, and that’s okay. The world needs athletes, artists, musicians, and innovators just as much. The responsibility of parents and educators is to nurture these talents without forcing them into pre-defined moulds.

While academics are important, they should not be the only measure of intelligence or future success. With the right support system, customized learning approaches, and societal acceptance, extracurricular-driven children can achieve greatness in their own fields – without being labelled as academic failures.

Encouraging passion, fostering talent, and making informed career choices will ensure that every child finds success in a field where they truly belong.

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
What if your child’s true talent lies outside textbooks? Anuradha C writes about how parents and schools can nurture children’s skills in sports, music, and arts without compromising essential learning.

item.title: Getting ready to take on the world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

In today’s interconnected world, classrooms cannot operate in isolation from global trends and challenges. The rapid advancements in technology and increased movement of people across borders have created an environment where local and global realities are deeply intertwined. Schools now reflect this diversity more than ever with students from various racial, cultural, and linguistic backgrounds. This demands that teachers adapt strategies to meet these evolving needs and prepare students to navigate the complexities of a globalized world.

Integrating global competence: a three-step approach

To embed global competence into teaching practices, I recommend a simple three-step approach:

Step 1: Define the skills

Clearly identify the competencies students need to thrive in a globalized world. These might include critical thinking, cultural awareness, empathy, and problem-solving. By focusing on specific skills, teachers can create targeted learning opportunities.

Example: A math teacher could focus on developing students’ data interpretation skills while fostering their understanding of global health trends. For instance, students could examine data on vaccination rates across countries, analyze the factors influencing these rates, and discuss the implications for public health.

Step 2: Create engaging tasks

Design tasks that actively engage students in practicing these skills. Effective tasks connect academic concepts to real-world challenges, encouraging students to think critically and collaborate.

Example: Using geometry, students could explore urban planning by designing a sustainable city layout. They could calculate areas for green spaces, analyze traffic flow patterns, and consider environmental factors like reducing carbon footprints. This task not only reinforces mathematical concepts but also introduces students to sustainability and global urban challenges.

Step 3: Develop an assessment rubric

Assessment is crucial for measuring progress and ensuring that students internalize the skills and values they learn. A well-rounded assessment includes teacher evaluation, self-assessment, peer feedback, and reflections on real-world relevance.

Example: For a project on renewable energy, students could calculate the efficiency of solar panels using trigonometry. The assessment rubric could evaluate the accuracy of their calculations, their ability to present findings, and their reflections on how renewable energy can address global energy needs.

Real-world applications in math

Mathematics provides a powerful platform to connect students to global issues. Here are two examples.

Understanding climate change through graphs

Climate change is a pressing global challenge that can be explored through mathematical concepts like graphs. Students analyze real-world data, such as trends in global temperatures or carbon emissions to understand the mathematical relationships behind these issues.

Task: Students plot temperature changes over the last century on graphs, focusing on a specific region. They analyze the data to identify the trends, discuss contributing factors like deforestation or industrialization, and propose actionable solutions to mitigate climate change.

Impact: This activity not only reinforces graph skills but also fosters critical thinking and emotional engagement as students realize the tangible effects of environmental changes on communities worldwide.

Exploring global health through probability

Probability can be used to understand global health challenges, such as the spread of diseases. By examining real-world data, students can learn how mathematical models predict outcomes and inform policy decisions.

Task: Students analyze data on the probability of disease outbreaks in different regions. They create probability models to predict the spread of a disease based on factors like vaccination rates or population density. Students then discuss how mathematical predictions can guide public health strategies.

Impact: This task enhances students’ analytical skills and deepens their understanding of how mathematical tools contribute to solving global health challenges.

Assessment: Measuring growth in global competence

Assessment plays a pivotal role in fostering global competence. It ensures students engage deeply with the material and develop the necessary skills and values. A comprehensive assessment approach includes:

Teacher assessment: Evaluate the accuracy and clarity of students’ mathematical work, such as their graphs or calculations.

Self-assessment: Encourage students to reflect on their learning process and how the task influenced their understanding of global issues.

Peer assessment: Facilitate collaboration by having students review each other’s work and provide constructive feedback.

Reflection: Ask students to write about how the task changed their perspective on global challenges and their role in addressing them.

Example: In a task exploring water scarcity, students could calculate their daily water usage and compare it with global averages. The assessment could include evaluating their calculations, their ability to present solutions for reducing water waste, and their reflections on the importance of water conservation.

Emotional engagement and real-world relevance

To truly foster global competence, lessons must evoke an emotional response. When students analyze climate data, they may feel a sense of urgency about environmental sustainability. Similarly, examining global health data can spark empathy for affected communities. These emotional connections make learning meaningful and inspire students to take action.

Example: After studying renewable energy, students could design a campaign to promote solar energy in their communities. This project not only reinforces their learning but also empowers them to make a tangible difference.

Conclusion

Fostering global competence is not just about preparing students for academic success, it’s about equipping them to contribute meaningfully to a globalized society. By embedding global competence into teaching, we help students become informed, responsible citizens, ready to address the challenges of an interconnected world.

References

  • Wagner, Parkhouse, and Glazier (2019).
  • Asia Society and OECD (2018).

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr. and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Climate change, global health, and math: Monica Kochar writes about real-world applications that make math learning both engaging and meaningful.

item.title: From the ‘world’ to the ‘word’: a Freirian look at reading
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Amruta Veer and Swati Jain

Introducing children to reading is a complex and demanding task for parents and teachers. There have been long-standing ideological debates over the most effective methods to teach reading to young children. The 'reading wars’, as they were called, oscillated between the phonics1 method and the whole language approach.2 Today, these debates have narrowed down to a balanced and integrated approach to literacy along with an emphasis on the context of the text and the direct inclusion of aspects from the child’s surroundings.

The introduction of this approach to reading instruction raises important questions for educators. What is the importance of a child's surroundings and the context of the text in acquiring reading skills? How do the surroundings affect comprehension of the text and how does the comprehension interact with the child's existing knowledge?

We looked at Freire’s seminal text, The Importance of the Act of Reading (1983), which discusses the nature of reading and its central role in literacy development. Freire situated reading within the broader context of understanding the world before decoding the text for meaning.

“Reading the world precedes reading the word. And reading the word implies continually reading the world.” (Freire, 1983, p. 10)

In his view, the child’s surroundings, experiences and observations form their ‘world’ or the context. The language of the adults around the child, their ideas, beliefs, and various aspects of daily life also contribute to it. The child should understand and decipher this ‘world’ before they try to decode written texts. However, this is not a linear process (Fig. 1). The subsequent reading of the word cannot dispense with continually reading the world. He explains, “Reading is not exhausted merely by decoding the written word or written language, but rather anticipated by and extending into the knowledge of the world” (Ibid.)

As the word is decoded, its meaning expands the child’s understanding of the world, which had at the outset, helped her to decode it. Thus, reading the context and reading the text are intertwined.

The physical context and surroundings of the child influence the meaning a word holds for the child. For example, the word ‘tree’ can hold different meanings for children raised in a village versus those raised in a city. Both children perceive trees in their surroundings, see how people interact with them, and hear the word used by them. This forms their unique context for understanding ‘tree’. When the child encounters the word ‘tree’ in written language, she will not only decode the word but also understand its meaning pertaining to her individual context. For a child from a rural background, the tree might represent a place to play, climb, or find shade. She may even understand trees as important for farming, or for firewood, making trees an organic, integral part of their daily lives.

Whereas for a child from an urban background, a ‘tree’ would mean something limited and special, mostly confined to parks and sidewalks. She would hear about trees more as a part of a science lesson or stories, viewing it as a concept rather than a regular, familiar aspect of their lives. Both these children will decode the word ‘tree’ and construct their understanding based on what the tree signifies to them, in their individual contexts.

Freire proposes that establishing this relationship between the context and the text is crucial for reading (Fig. 2). Through this process, the word shaped by the reader’s individual context, acquires a unique meaning for her and truly becomes 'hers. 'Freire explains this process as ‘rewriting’ the word, as now it possesses an individual or personal significance for the reader.

These three processes – perception, interpretation of the written word, and its ‘rewriting’ in relation to the context form the basis of critical reading. Critical reading should alter and expand the child’s perception of the world. The child learns and evolves through this interaction. Freire further argues that reading is ‘the act of knowing’ and ‘the act of creativity’.

It is the creative task of the child to understand how the word ‘tree’ is constructed and learn to recognize, decode, and eventually write it.

Implications for parents and educators

Freire’s article compels us to think about the approaches to reading taken by schools which primarily focus on instruction and practice of mechanics of reading, neglecting the importance of the real-world context and experiences. He challenged the mechanical and reductive view of reading which advocates for phonics – firstinstruction before addressing critical comprehension. He considers teaching literacy through exercises like memorizing vowel sounds, akin to filling the ‘supposedly empty heads of learners’ with the teacher’s words. According to him, children’s curiosity should be fostered so that they find the ‘words’ embodied in objects, ideas, and signs around them and feel a sense of discovery in finding these words in written text.

Pre-school is the time when the child is mediating the school world and the home world. The pedagogy should help foster a connection between these worlds. ‘[With his teacher], reading the word never entailed a break with reading the world’ (Freire, 1983, p. 8). The key to maintaining this continuity is by encouraging the child to speak with spontaneity about experiences, learning to read texts, and writing words that come from a child's ideas and observations and not from a textbook or the teacher’s ‘world’. This can be achieved by letting the child’s everyday reality seep into the teaching process. It can form the basis of the context required to look at the written word meaningfully as well as nudge the child towards learning the formal rules of reading while keeping the spontaneity of the process alive. Educators and parents should be mindful to further this exploration so the child’s journey toward literacy is rooted in their everyday actions and musings, while maintaining an active dialogue with the child about these experiences. In this way, their journey from the ‘world’ to the ‘word’ becomes a more organic one.

Freire also emphasizes that the vocabulary used in a literacy program must come from the learners' own language universe, reflecting their real language, anxieties, fears, needs, and aspirations. Surveying this ‘word universe’ provides words that are deeply connected to their lived experiences. This has significant implications for teaching children from marginalized communities, who often do not encounter their home language in the school setting. In a similar vein, Sylvia Ashton Warner, in the 1970s, developed a literacy method for teaching Māori children in New Zealand. Instead of relying on prescribed texts, learners were encouraged to engage with written culture on their own terms. They were taught words necessary to their lives and encouraged to write sentences and stories to share with other learners. ‘No time is too long spent talking to a child to find out his key words, the key that unlocks himself, for in them is the secret of reading, the realisation that words can have intense meaning. Words having no emotional significance to him, no instinctive meaning, could be an imposition, doing him more harm than not teaching him at all. They may teach him that words mean nothing, and that reading is undesirable.’ (Ashton-Warner, 1972, p.44)

In this context, it is also essential to reflect on the true meaning of being "literate." The Ministry of Education (MOE) has defined it as the ability to read, write, and compute with comprehension (Porecha, 2024). However, is it adequate to simply teach children to sign their names and accurately read and write simple texts? Or should we aim for a more comprehensive definition of literacy – where children comprehend what they read and write, recognize its significance in their lives, and are equipped to critically engage with and navigate the world of written texts? (Menon, 2015)

Hellen Keller’s (2012) account of learning the connection between the ‘word’ – water and ‘the cool, wonderful something that was flowing over her hand’ reveals very poignantly how the mystery of language unfolded before her. The joy and hope which came from this ‘living word’ is palpable in this autobiographical text.

“Everything had a name and each name gave birth to a new thought. As we returned to the house, every object which I touched seemed to quiver with life. That was because I saw everything with the strange, new sight that had come to me” (Keller, 2012).

This serves as an important reminder of the significance of the relationship between words, written texts, and the external world for a child. Learning to read brings this connection to life. As educators, it is crucial to recognize and strengthen this connection in teaching practices.

1 A science-based method where students sound out words to understand the text. Phonics help students read unfamiliar words on their own.

2 The whole language approach believes learning to read should be as natural as learning to speak. Children learn to read by guessing unfamiliar words and using written clues to figure them out.

References

  • Ashton-Warner, S. (1972). "Teacher" in America. Cassell.
  • Freire, P. (1983). The importance of the act of reading. Journal of education, 165(1), 5-11.
  • Freire, P. (1985). Reading the world and reading the word: An interview with Paulo Freire. Language Arts, 62(1), 15-21.
  • Keller, H., Sullivan, A., & Macy, J.A. (2012). Story of My Life: By Helen Keller. Wilder Pub.
  • Jayaram, K. (2012). Towards a Conceptual Framework for Early Literacy: A Balanced and Socially Sensitive Approach. Language and Language Teaching, 1(1), 32-39.
  • Menon, S. (2015). Baal Sahitya Ke Zariye Prarambhik Bhasha Evam Saksharta Ko Seenchna. Bhasha Boli (1), 3-9.
  • Porecha (2024, August 26). Education Ministry defines ‘literacy’, ‘full literacy’. The Hindu. https://www.thehindu.com/news/national/education-ministry-defines-literacy-full-literacy/arti- cle68569537.ece

Amruta Veer is a special needs educator with ChildRaise Trust, a Mumbai-based NGO dedicated to empowering individuals with disabilities. Passionate about inclusive education, she primarily focuses on interventions for specific learning disabilities and literacy development in young children. She can be reached at amrutasveer@gmail.com.

Swati Jain is a doctoral student at the Centre of Excellence in Teacher Education at the Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai. She has taught at the primary level for many years and takes a keen interest in language and literacy education. She can be reached at gswatijain@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Amruta Veer and Swati Jain
Should children read textbooks – or the world around them? Amruta Veer and Swati Jain write about how reading can be an act of discovery, not only of the written word, but also of the world that surrounds the child.
